section.title: Abraham Pleads for Sodom
section.sectionRationale: Having confirmed the promise, the LORD lets Abraham in on His purpose to judge Sodom, treating him as a friend and a chosen intercessor for the nations. Abraham's bold, humble bargaining probes the justice and mercy of God and models intercession that appeals to God's own righteousness.
section.thesis: The Judge of all the earth invites His chosen friend to plead, and Abraham intercedes for the wicked on the ground of the righteous few, learning that God will spare a whole city for the sake of a remnant and will always do what is right.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the LORD resolves to disclose His purpose to Abraham; and Abraham's descending appeal from fifty to ten righteous ones.

units.label: Shall I hide from Abraham?
units.note: the LORD confides in His friend

verses.text: When the men got up to leave, they looked out over Sodom, and Abraham walked along with them to see them off.
And the LORD said, “Shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do?
Abraham will surely become a great and powerful nation, and through him all the nations of the earth will be blessed.

For I have chosen him, so that he will command his children and his household after him to keep the way of the LORD by doing what is right and just, in order that the LORD may bring upon Abraham what He has promised.”
Then the LORD said, “The outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is great. Because their sin is so grievous,
I will go down to see if their actions fully justify the outcry that has reached Me. If not, I will find out.”

units.label: Will not the Judge do right?

verses.text: And the two men turned away and went toward Sodom, but Abraham remained standing before the LORD.
Abraham stepped forward and said, “Will You really sweep away the {{tsaddiq|righteous}} with the wicked?
What if there are fifty righteous ones in the city? Will You really sweep it away and not spare the place for the sake of the fifty righteous ones who are there?

{{chalilah|Far be it}} from You to do such a thing, to kill the righteous with the wicked, so that the righteous and the wicked are treated alike. Far be it from You! Will not the {{shaphat|Judge}} of all the earth do what is right?”
So the LORD replied, “If I find fifty righteous ones within the city of Sodom, on their account I will spare the whole place.”
Then Abraham answered, “Now that I have ventured to speak to the Lord, though I am but dust and ashes,
suppose the fifty righteous ones lack five. Will You destroy the whole city for the lack of five?” He replied, “If I find forty-five there, I will not destroy it.”
Once again Abraham spoke to the LORD, “Suppose forty are found there?” He answered, “On account of the forty, I will not do it.”
Then Abraham said, “May the Lord not be angry, but let me speak further. Suppose thirty are found there?” He replied, “If I find thirty there, I will not do it.”
And Abraham said, “Now that I have ventured to speak to the Lord, suppose twenty are found there?” He answered, “On account of the twenty, I will not destroy it.”
Finally, Abraham said, “May the Lord not be angry, but let me speak once more. Suppose ten are found there?” And He answered, “On account of the ten, I will not destroy it.”
When the LORD had finished speaking with Abraham, He departed, and Abraham returned home.

tsaddiq.gloss: Righteous, just, in the right. Abraham's whole plea turns on the presence of the righteous within a wicked city, and on whether God will treat them the same as the guilty. The word raises the question of how the just and the unjust can be dealt with rightly.

chalilah.gloss: Far be it, an expression of horror at something unthinkable, almost profane. Abraham uses it twice, appealing to God's own character: it would be unthinkable for the righteous Judge to slay the righteous with the wicked. He argues with God on the basis of who God is.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, govern, decide, vindicate. The Judge of all the earth is the ruler who alone has the right and wisdom to condemn and to acquit. Abraham's confidence rests on the certainty that this Judge will do what is right, that His justice can be trusted.

basic.context: As the visitors head toward Sodom, the LORD chooses to tell Abraham what He is about to do, treating him as a friend and as the one through whom the nations will be blessed. Abraham then pleads for the city. The key question is out loud in verse 25: will not the Judge of all the earth do what is right?
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real conversation, and it teaches us how to intercede: boldly yet humbly, appealing to God's own character. The main point is God's perfect justice joined to His readiness to spare for the sake of the righteous. Readers ask whether Abraham changed God's mind or was drawn into God's own heart, but either way God's justice is vindicated.
basic.originalLanguages: The whole plea turns on the righteous, the tsaddiq, and whether they will be swept away with the wicked. Abraham twice cries chalilah, far be it, an appeal to God's character. He trusts the shaphat, the Judge of all the earth, to do right. His argument is not against God's justice but from it.
basic.typology: God's willingness to spare the many for the sake of a righteous few points forward to the one righteous One, Christ, for whose sake a whole people is spared. Abraham standing before the LORD to plead prefigures the great Intercessor.
basic.christ: Where ten righteous could not be found in Sodom, God provides one perfectly righteous Man, Jesus, and for His sake spares all who are His. Christ is the Intercessor who ever lives to plead for His people, greater than Abraham.
basic.god: God is perfectly just, never treating the righteous and the wicked alike, and He is merciful, ready to spare a whole city for a remnant. He is humble enough to confide in a friend and to be pleaded with.
basic.crossReferences: James calls Abraham the friend of God. Ezekiel 22 has God seeking one to stand in the gap. Romans 5 shows how the righteousness of one brings life to many.
basic.oneStory: Here God reveals His justice and His mercy together and invites His friend to intercede. The pattern of sparing the many for the righteous few reaches its fulfillment in Christ, the one righteous One for whose sake God spares a countless people from judgment.

context.literary: The passage bridges the promise scene and the destruction of Sodom in chapter 19. It is built on divine soliloquy, shall I hide from Abraham, followed by a carefully patterned dialogue that descends by fives and then tens from fifty to ten, each round more deferential than the last.
context.historical: Sodom's notorious wickedness had produced an outcry, the same word used for the cry of the oppressed. Intercession by a patriarch or prophet on behalf of a people was a recognized role, and Abraham here acts as the mediating figure through whom nations are to be blessed.
context.canonical: This is the Bible's first sustained intercession, setting a pattern later seen in Moses, Samuel, and the prophets, and it raises the question of the righteous remnant that runs through Scripture to its answer in the one righteous One, Jesus Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal God's justice and mercy in judging the wicked while sparing the righteous, to establish Abraham as intercessor for the nations, and to vindicate the trustworthiness of the Judge of all the earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The dialogue describes a unique moment, yet it models a prescriptive pattern of humble, bold intercession grounded in God's character. The abiding truth is that God's justice is perfect and His mercy real.

debates.question: Did Abraham change God's mind, or was he drawn into God's own purpose?

views.label: Genuine intercession that moves God to relent
views.case: The text reads as a real dialogue in which God responds to each request, showing that God ordains prayer as a true means by which His mercy is expressed.

views.label: God leading Abraham to discover His own heart
views.case: Since God already intends to be just and merciful, the descending questions teach Abraham, and us, the character of the Judge rather than altering an unchanging God.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that God's justice is never in doubt and that intercession matters; the passage encourages bold prayer that rests on who God is.

study.originalLanguages: Abraham's plea centers on the tsaddiq, the righteous, and the fear that they will be swept away with the wicked. Twice he exclaims chalilah, far be it, a word of horror that appeals directly to God's character: such a thing would be unthinkable for God. His confidence is in the shaphat kol-haaretz, the Judge of all the earth, who by definition must do what is right. The argument runs not against God's justice but out of it.

typology.type: A whole city spared for the sake of the righteous few
typology.fulfillment: A people spared for the sake of the one righteous One, Christ, whose obedience justifies many, Romans 5:18-19.

typology.type: Abraham standing before the LORD to intercede
typology.fulfillment: Christ the great High Priest who ever lives to make intercession for His people, Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: The search for a righteous remnant in a doomed city
typology.fulfillment: The gospel truth that where none is righteous, God provides His own righteous One, Romans 3:10-24.

study.christ: The logic of the passage, that God would spare the many for the sake of the righteous, finds its answer in Christ. In Sodom not even ten righteous could be found, and Scripture confesses that none is righteous, no not one; yet God provides one perfectly righteous Man, and for His sake spares a countless multitude from the judgment they deserve, Romans 5:18-19. Abraham standing before the LORD to plead foreshadows Jesus, the greater Intercessor, who ever lives to make intercession for those who draw near to God through Him, Hebrews 7:25.
study.god: God is revealed as perfectly just, one who will never treat the righteous and the wicked alike, and as genuinely merciful, willing to spare an entire wicked city for the sake of a small remnant. He is humble enough to take a friend into His confidence and patient enough to be pleaded with six times over. His justice is not cold severity but the reliable rightness on which Abraham stakes his whole appeal.

crossReferences.note: Abraham was called the friend of God, fitting the LORD's choice to confide His purpose in him.
God looks for one to stand in the gap on behalf of the land, the role Abraham fills here.
As one man's righteousness leads to justification for many, the pattern of the many spared for the righteous.
God remembered Abraham and brought Lot out, showing his intercession was not in vain.

study.oneStory: God discloses His justice and mercy together and draws His friend into intercession for the wicked on behalf of the righteous few. The pattern points beyond itself: where no righteous remnant can be found, God supplies the one righteous One, Christ, for whose sake He spares a people beyond counting. Abraham's bold, humble pleading foreshadows the greater Intercessor who ever lives to pray for His own.

quiz.q: What is the great question at the heart of Abraham's plea?

options.text: Will not the Judge of all the earth do what is right?
options.note: Abraham stakes his whole appeal on God's perfect justice.

options.text: Will God spare Lot no matter what?
options.note: Lot is in view, but the explicit question is about God's justice for all.

options.text: Why does God allow evil cities to exist?
options.note: That is not the question the text raises here.

options.text: Can Abraham become more righteous than Sodom?
options.note: Abraham calls himself dust and ashes; the question is about God's justice, not his own merit.

quiz.q: On what basis does Abraham plead for Sodom?

options.text: On the presence of the righteous, asking God to spare the city for their sake.
options.note: He appeals to the righteous few within the wicked city.

options.text: On the argument that Sodom was not really that sinful.
options.note: He never disputes their guilt; he appeals to the righteous within.

options.text: On his own good works and standing before God.
options.note: He calls himself dust and ashes; his plea rests on the righteous, not himself.

options.text: On the claim that God has no right to judge.
options.note: He affirms God's right to judge; he appeals to God's justice and mercy.

quiz.q: Why does the LORD choose to tell Abraham His plans?

options.text: Because Abraham is chosen and through him the nations will be blessed.
options.note: God treats Abraham as a friend and covenant partner.

options.text: Because God needed Abraham's permission to act.
options.note: God confides in him as a friend, but does not require his permission.

options.text: Because Abraham had demanded to know.
options.note: God volunteers the information; Abraham does not demand it.

options.text: Because Abraham lived in Sodom.
options.note: Abraham lived at Mamre; it was Lot who lived in Sodom.

quiz.q: How far did the LORD agree to go in sparing the city?

options.text: He agreed to spare it for the sake of as few as ten righteous ones.
options.note: The dialogue descends from fifty to ten.

options.text: He agreed to spare it only if fifty righteous were found.
options.note: Fifty was the starting point; He went all the way down to ten.

options.text: He refused to spare it under any condition.
options.note: He repeatedly agreed to spare it for the righteous.

options.text: He promised to spare it for the sake of one righteous person.
options.note: The dialogue stops at ten, not one.

quiz.q: How does this scene point to Christ?

options.text: Where no righteous remnant is found, God provides one righteous One for whose sake many are spared.
options.note: Christ's righteousness justifies many, Romans 5.

options.text: It shows that cities can always save themselves by good citizens.
options.note: The deeper pointer is to the one righteous One, not civic virtue.

options.text: It teaches that judgment never actually falls.
options.note: Judgment does fall on Sodom; mercy is shown through the righteous.

options.text: It proves that intercession is pointless.
options.note: Abraham's intercession foreshadows Christ's effective intercession.

questions: God confided in Abraham as a friend before acting. What does it mean to you that God draws His people close rather than keeping them at a distance?
Abraham interceded boldly yet humbly, calling himself 'dust and ashes.' Who is God laying on your heart to pray for, and how can you plead for them?
Abraham anchored his prayer in God's character: 'Will not the Judge of all the earth do what is right?' How does trusting God's justice help you when the world seems unfair?
God was willing to spare a whole city for the sake of a righteous few. How does that shape the way you see the value of faithful believers in a hard place?
In Christ, God provides the one righteous One for whose sake many are spared. How does that truth give you confidence to draw near to God today?

translationNotes.note: Outcry translates a word used for the cry of the oppressed; Sodom's sin has provoked a cry that reaches heaven.
Far be it from You renders an idiom of horror at the unthinkable; Abraham appeals to God's own character, not merely to an outcome.
The Judge of all the earth uses a verb meaning to judge, govern, and vindicate; it affirms God's sovereign right and perfect rightness in judgment.
